name: Benefits-Led vs Feature-Led Narrative Architecture

fields.definition_origin: The benefits-vs-features distinction predates onboarding research — it traces to direct-response copywriting (Eugene Schwartz, 'Breakthrough Advertising' 1966; David Ogilvy, 'Confessions of an Advertising Man' 1963) and the marketing axiom 'sell the hole, not the drill' (often attributed to Theodore Levitt, Harvard Marketing Myopia 1960). Donald Miller's StoryBrand (2017) re-popularized it for product copy. Applied to onboarding by Andrew Chen, Reforge, and Appcues throughout 2018-2024 as 'value-first onboarding'. The PDF Deep Dive cites Blinkist's 'Understand powerful ideas in 15 minutes' (outcome) vs the feature-led alternative 'Thousands of book summaries' (inventory) as the canonical contrast — same product, opposite framing, materially different conversion.
fields.core_mechanism: Operates via construal-level theory (Trope & Liberman 2010) and means-end chain theory (Gutman 1982). Users do not buy product features (low-construal); they buy desired end-states (high-construal). When onboarding leads with features, the user must mentally construct the means-end chain themselves — adding cognitive load (Sweller 1988) and creating opportunity for the user to mis-infer the benefit. When onboarding leads with benefits, the chain is pre-resolved: the user immediately maps the product to a stressor they want to resolve. Mechanism stack: (1) outcome-framed copy activates pre-existing motivational schemas (the user already cares about the outcome — they just need to learn this product reaches it); (2) reduces cognitive load by pre-completing the means-end inference; (3) emotional engagement is higher for end-states than capabilities (Damasio 1994 — somatic markers attach to outcomes, not technical attributes); (4) anti-AI-slop posture — feature-led copy is the AI-codegen default, benefits-led is the discipline.
fields.neuroscience_correlate: Outcome/benefit framing engages ventromedial prefrontal cortex (subjective value coding) and orbitofrontal cortex (reward anticipation). Feature framing engages dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (analytic processing) — heavier load, lower emotional pull. Storytelling/outcome framing produces neural coupling between speaker and listener (Stephens, Silbert, Hasson 2010 fMRI). [uncertain on direct mobile-onboarding fMRI]
fields.empirical_evidence: (1) Blinkist's 'Understand powerful ideas in 15 minutes' messaging (PDF Deep Dive cite) is the canonical case — reportedly outconverts feature-led variants in their internal A/B tests (Blinkist team blog posts, 2019-2022). (2) Headspace's 2024 onboarding leads with 'Stress less. Sleep better. Live happier.' (outcome trio) — reported ~70% completion rate (Tear Them Down teardown). (3) Calm uses outcome-led: 'Get more restful sleep' / 'Reduce anxiety' as section openers. (4) Cal AI: 'Reach your goal weight' (outcome) not 'AI-powered macro tracker' (feature). (5) Ogilvy's 'At 60 miles an hour the loudest noise in this new Rolls-Royce comes from the electric clock' is feature copy that succeeds because the feature IS the benefit (silence = luxury) — feature-led only works when the feature directly equals the desired outcome. (6) PDF Deep Dive: 'Users are indifferent to algorithms or technology stacks; they care about the resolution of personal stressors.' (7) Reforge curriculum (Casey Winters): outcome-led onboarding outperforms feature-led across 100+ teardowns. (8) StoryBrand framework deployed across 1000s of B2B/B2C apps with documented retention/conversion lifts.
fields.measurable_outcome_metric: Onboarding completion rate (especially first-3-screen completion — the benefits arc lives there), trial-start conversion at paywall, copy-A/B winner rate (top-decile A/B programs replace feature copy with benefit copy as their highest-volume win), Adapty 2026: localization win rate at 62.3% — most localization wins are reframing feature copy as benefit copy in target locale. Brand recall in first-session exit interview (qualitative).
fields.evidence_strength: industry_benchmark + practitioner_opinion (foundational marketing theory peer-reviewed; specific app-level RCTs are case studies)
fields.conversion_lift_benchmark: Generic copy-test benchmark: replacing feature-led headlines with benefit-led typically yields 10-30% lift in onboarding completion and 5-15% lift in trial conversion (PostHog/Mixpanel A/B test compilations 2024-2025). Adapty 2026 paywall: outcome-framed paywall headlines outperform feature-list paywall headlines in 60%+ of localization tests. Specific RCT figures are unverified at the app level but the pattern is strong industry consensus.
fields.engineering_tactics: (1) Define an outcome lexicon per app: 3-5 phrases capturing what the user gets (NOT what the app does). For Cal AI: 'Reach your goal weight', 'Track without thinking', 'Eat what you love'. For Headspace: 'Stress less', 'Sleep better', 'Live happier'. (2) Author copy as 'Means-End': for every screen, write the FEATURE, then ask 'so that what?' twice — the second answer is the benefit copy. (3) Forbid technical jargon in onboarding: AI/algorithm/ML/machine-learning/personalization/recommendation are all feature words; replace with the outcome ('a plan made for you' not 'AI-personalized plan'). (4) Architecture: store copy as a structured object with `{ feature: string, benefit: string, outcome: string }` triplets — the screen renders only the benefit/outcome but the schema preserves the chain. (5) Internationalization layer must translate BENEFITS not features verbatim — outcome resonance is locale-specific. (6) Each major screen has a single OUTCOME headline (5-9 words, present tense, second person preferred). (7) Social-proof copy supports outcome ('Join 5M people who sleep better' not '5M downloads'). (8) Paywall copy MUST be outcome-led — feature-list paywalls underperform side-by-side outcome paywalls. (9) Telemetry: A/B test framework that allows rapid headline-copy variant rollout via server-driven UI (item 31).
fields.real_world_examples: (1) Blinkist (PDF Deep Dive cite): 'Understand powerful ideas in 15 minutes' outcome opener, NOT 'Thousands of book summaries' inventory. (2) Headspace 2024 onboarding (Mobbin teardown): 'Stress less. Sleep better. Live happier.' as the affirm act. (3) Calm 2024: 'Soften your sleep' / 'Untangle your thoughts'. (4) Strava: 'Beat yesterday' (outcome — not 'GPS tracking with social feed'). (5) Duolingo: 'Free, fun, and effective language learning' — three benefits stacked. (6) Notion: 'A new tool that blends your everyday work apps into one' — feature-leaning, but feature IS the benefit (consolidation = the outcome). (7) Cal AI screen 1: 'Reach your goal weight without thinking about it' (outcome) NOT 'AI calorie tracking'. (8) Mau Baron Prayer Lock: 'Lock in your daily prayer practice' (outcome) NOT 'AI-generated personalized prayers'. (9) Mojo: 'Make your reels go viral' (outcome) NOT 'animated templates and effects'.
fields.mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile-primary in 2024-2026 — mobile attention budget is shorter, so outcome framing matters more. Web has more headline real estate so feature-supporting copy can sit below the outcome headline (hero pattern: outcome headline + 2-3 feature bullets below). Mobile copy must compress outcomes to ≤9 words; web tolerates 12-18 word outcome statements with a sub-headline expansion.
fields.platform_design_language_alignment: Liquid Glass's bolder, left-aligned alert/onboarding typography is purpose-built for outcome headlines. M3 Expressive's expressive type system also rewards outcome-led headlines visually. Both DLs reward CONFIDENT typography — outcome statements feel right at this scale, feature lists feel cluttered.
fields.category_fit: Universal but with calibration: wellness/habit (heavy benefit framing — 'sleep better'), AI-wrapper (must restrain feature-flexing — 'a plan for you' not 'GPT-4 powered'), fintech (benefit-led with regulatory truth-in-advertising constraint — 'grow your savings' not 'auto-invest spare change' is preferable when honest), B2B/productivity (outcome-led but feature-grounded — 'one place for your work' is benefit-led but means-end short), gaming (outcomes are 'win/explore/build' not 'physics engine'), social (benefits are 'find your people' not 'algorithmic feed'). EXCLUSIONS: regulated fintech disclosures, KYC/identity verification (must be feature-explicit for compliance).
fields.solo_dev_feasibility: same_day. Copy rewriting is the lowest-cost highest-leverage onboarding work. Audit existing screens, run each headline through 'so what?' twice, ship benefit-led variants. The hardest part is internal discipline — engineers and designers default to feature copy.
fields.tooling_options: Server-driven UI (Plotline, Lovi protocol) for rapid copy A/B; PostHog / Statsig / Eppo for headline experiment infra; Crowdin / Lokalise for outcome-localized copy; Lex / Copy.ai / Anthropic API for outcome-copy generation (with human review). RevenueCat / Adapty paywall builders allow per-locale outcome variant testing.
fields.ai_dependency: none required; optional for copy generation. Frontier-class LLMs (Claude Opus / GPT-4o) can transform feature lists into outcome statements via 'so what?' chain prompting. Small models tend to revert to feature framing — use frontier models for this transformation. Always human-review LLM-generated copy for accuracy and tone.
fields.claude_design_application: Instruct Claude Design to author screen copy as outcome statements only. Prompt fragment: 'For each onboarding screen, generate ONE headline (5-9 words, present-tense, second-person if appropriate) that names the OUTCOME the user achieves, not the feature the app provides. Apply the means-end test: write the feature, ask "so that what?" twice; the second answer is the headline. Forbid words: AI, algorithm, machine learning, ML, personalization, recommendation, smart, intelligent, advanced, powered by. If you must reference technology, embed it as a reassurance bullet under the outcome headline (not in the headline). Output a `CopyTriplet { feature, benefit, outcome }` for every screen, render only the outcome on the screen but preserve the triplet for localization.'
fields.stitch_application: Prompt fragment: 'For each mockup screen, the headline must be an OUTCOME (5-9 words, present-tense, second-person where natural). Examples to follow: "Sleep better tonight" / "Reach your goal weight" / "Lock in your daily practice" / "Find your people". Examples to AVOID: "AI-powered habit tracker" / "Personalized recommendations" / "Smart calorie tracking". Headline: 32px Cabinet Grotesk Bold, left-aligned. Sub-headline (optional, 17px Söhne Regular, max 18 words) may expand the outcome but must not introduce technology jargon. Forbid the words: AI, algorithm, ML, smart, intelligent, advanced. Forbid feature inventories ("Track | Plan | Achieve" three-column layouts).'
fields.ai_codegen_failure_mode: AI codegen leans HEAVILY feature-led by default — it has been trained on tech marketing copy and reverts to 'AI-powered', 'machine learning', 'smart recommendations' framings. Common failures: (1) headline is the technology ('AI-powered nutrition coach' not 'Eat better effortlessly'), (2) three-column feature grid 'Track | Plan | Achieve' replaces single outcome headline, (3) 'powered by' badges proliferate, (4) every benefit reverts to a feature on copy review. Counter-prompt: 'CONSTRAINT: every onboarding headline must pass the means-end test. Write the feature, ask "so that what?" twice, use the second answer as the headline. Banned words in headlines: AI, algorithm, ML, smart, intelligent, advanced, powered, automated, precise, personalized. The word personalized must be replaced with "made for you" or omitted. Verify every screen headline against the banned list before output.' Also: AI-slop tells (per .agent/research/emotional-design/results/AI_Slop_Tells.json) — indigo-500 + Lucide trinity + feature inventory triple-stack is the slop signature; explicitly forbid.
fields.anti_patterns: (1) Feature-list onboarding ('Track. Plan. Achieve.' three-column grid) — feels like a brochure. (2) Technology bragging ('Powered by GPT-4', 'AI-personalized', 'Machine-learning calibrated') — readers don't care; feature-led AI-slop. (3) Benefit-washing — claiming an outcome the product doesn't deliver ('Lose weight effortlessly' for a basic calorie counter) — FTC § 5 deceptive-practice exposure. (4) Outcome-stacking with no proof — three benefit headlines in a row with no demo or social proof feels hollow. (5) Mismatched register — corporate Sage outcome copy on a Jester archetype app reads as inauthentic. (6) Feature-led paywall after benefit-led onboarding — broken narrative chain (item 42 alignment).
fields.regulatory_risk: Medium. FTC § 5 deceptive-practice rule applies to outcome claims in onboarding — every 'Sleep better' / 'Lose weight' / 'Save money' headline must be substantiable. Health/wellness outcomes especially scrutinized (FDA + FTC overlap for medical-leaning claims). EU Unfair Commercial Practices Directive + Digital Fairness Act (Q4 2026 draft) will scrutinize outcome claims that overstate. Apple App Store Guideline 2.3.1 (accurate metadata) — outcome-led App Store screenshots must align with in-app delivery. UK ASA / Australian ACCC have pursued health-app outcome claims aggressively in 2024-2025.
fields.platform_constraints_2026: Apple App Store 2.3.1 (accurate metadata) — onboarding outcome claims must align with App Store description claims. Apple HIG: prefer 'human, friendly' tone — outcome-led aligns. Android Play Store policy on misleading claims similar. EU Digital Services Act labeling rules for in-app health/financial outcome claims tightening 2025-2026.
fields.accessibility_implications: Outcome-led copy is generally MORE accessible than feature-led — shorter, more concrete, easier for screen-reader users to parse. WCAG 2.2: 3.1.5 Reading Level — outcome statements typically score lower (more readable). Avoid metaphor-heavy outcome copy that obscures meaning for cognitive-disability users ('Untangle your thoughts' is a metaphor; provide concrete sub-headline). VoiceOver: outcome headline becomes the screen's primary landmark — give it explicit heading semantic.
fields.pitfalls_caveats: (1) Outcome promises must be substantiable — FTC will pursue (item regulatory_risk). (2) Outcome headlines that are too abstract ('Transform your life') feel hollow; specificity wins ('Sleep 30 minutes longer'). (3) Localization is non-trivial — outcome resonance varies by locale (Adapty 2026 62.3% localization win rate is largely about getting outcome framing locale-correct). (4) Outcome inflation — over-promising leads to user disappointment in first session, churn, refund spike. (5) Feature copy is sometimes correct: in B2B / technical categories where the buyer IS evaluating capability (Linear, Stripe, Notion for power users), feature mention is necessary — embed under outcome, don't replace.
fields.cross_cultural_validity: JP/KR: outcome framing tends toward modesty — 'Sleep a little better' rather than 'Transform your sleep'; aggressive outcome claims read as untrustworthy. CN: outcome + social-proof bundling ('5M+ people sleep better with us'). LATAM: warm outcome framing + family/community framing ('your family sleeps better'). MENA: faith-aligned outcome framing for relevant categories (Prayer Lock case). RTL: outcome headline left-align flips to right-align; preserve the outcome-first hierarchy. Adapty 2026 documented 62.3% LTV win rate for localization — most lift comes from outcome reframing per locale.
fields.prescribed_action: Replace every feature-led headline in onboarding with an outcome-led headline that passes the means-end test (write feature, ask 'so that what?' twice, second answer is the headline). Restrict headlines to 5-9 words, present-tense, second-person where natural. Forbid the words: AI, algorithm, ML, smart, intelligent, advanced, powered, personalized in any headline. Localize OUTCOMES not just translate features. Preserve a `CopyTriplet { feature, benefit, outcome }` schema for every screen.
fields.questionnaire_relevance: Question copy itself benefits from outcome framing — 'What's your goal weight?' (outcome) over 'Enter target weight (kg)' (feature/data). The QUESTION HEADER per question should reference the outcome the user is moving toward, not the data being collected.
fields.aha_relevance: Direct — the AI plan reveal lands harder when prior screens have framed in outcome language. If onboarding screens 1-N are feature-led, the reveal at step N+1 has to context-switch the user's framing. Outcome-led onboarding pre-loads the outcome schema so the reveal is a fulfillment, not a surprise.
fields.measurement_horizon: Day_0 (onboarding completion + paywall conversion) and Day_30 (substantiation — did the user actually achieve the outcome promise? feeds into refund and review analysis)
fields.emotional_archetype_alignment: Universal — every archetype benefits from outcome framing, but each has its outcome lexicon: Magician ('transform'), Caregiver ('feel cared for', 'rest easy'), Hero ('overcome', 'achieve'), Sage ('understand', 'see clearly'), Lover ('connect', 'belong'), Outlaw ('break free'), Jester ('have fun', 'play'), Innocent ('feel safe'), Explorer ('discover'), Creator ('make'), Ruler ('command', 'master'), Everyman ('belong'). Pair outcome lexicon to archetype voice so framing feels native.
fields.anti_pattern_paired: Pairs against AI-Slop tells (feature-jargon proliferation, indigo-500 + Lucide trinity + 'AI-powered' headline cluster). Pairs against Wall-of-Text + Cognitive Overload (Six-Antipattern #1) when feature-led copy dumps inventory. Pairs against Mismatched Archetype Voice (modern antipattern) when outcome lexicon doesn't match archetype.
sources: [Strategic Architecture and Psychological Frameworks for Mobile Application Onboarding (project PDF, Blinkist cite)](.agent/Deep%20Dive%20Into%20App%20Onboarding.pdf)

uncertain: specific A/B test conversion lift figures at the app level (industry pattern strong; per-app RCTs are unpublished)
neuroscience_correlate (foundational fMRI exists but not in mobile-onboarding specifically)
